summary:
The Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth (Rzeczpospolita, 1569–1795 AD) was a vast elective monarchy spanning much of central-eastern Europe — at its peak covering modern-day Poland, Lithuania, Latvia, Belarus, Ukraine, and parts of Russia — that represented one of early modern Europe's most unusual political experiments: a federated republic of the nobility (szlachta) in which the king was elected by some 500,000–1,000,000 noble voters, governed through a bicameral Diet (Sejm), and constrained by noble rights so extensive that they ultimately proved the state's fatal weakness. The Union of Lublin (1569), which formally merged the Kingdom of Poland and the Grand Duchy of Lithuania into a single political entity, created the largest state in Europe at its formation.

The Commonwealth's distinctive political culture celebrated noble freedom (złota wolność, 'golden liberty') to an extreme: the liberum veto, introduced in 1652, allowed any single nobleman to dissolve the Sejm and nullify all its legislation — a device so destructive that foreign powers (Russia, Prussia, Austria) routinely bribed Polish nobles to exercise it, paralyzing government. The Commonwealth achieved remarkable cultural and religious tolerance: it sheltered Ashkenazi Jews (who found nowhere else in Europe so hospitable), Protestant minorities, and Orthodox Christians, earning the description 'a state without stakes' for its absence of religious executions. Its periods of military glory (Jan Sobieski's relief of [[event:2415|Vienna]], 1683) coexisted with structural political weakness. The [[event:2334|Partitions of Poland]] (1772, 1793, 1795) erased the state entirely; the [[event:2334|Constitution of May 3, 1791]] — the first written national constitution in Europe, which abolished the liberum veto — came too late to save the Commonwealth from its neighbors.
